metadata.summary: The PACE framework for social media growth: choose one platform, define pillars, post consistently, engage daily. Master one platform first; track engagement rate, not follower count.

content.title: Social Media Strategy
content.summary: The PACE framework (Platform → Audience → Content → Engagement) for solopreneurs building an audience on social media. Master one platform before adding another; commit to six months before evaluating results. Track engagement rate, not follower count, as the primary KPI.

why.p: Most social media failures stem from platform sprawl, inconsistent cadence, and treating posts as broadcasts. The PACE framework forces sequencing: pick the platform where your ICP already spends time, define 3-5 content pillars, post on a fixed schedule, and spend daily time engaging. Engagement rate (likes + comments + shares / impressions) predicts content quality; follower count is a lagging indicator that follows engagement, not the other way around.

list.item: Choosing which platform(s) to invest in for a new product or personal brand
Designing a content calendar with pillar distribution and posting frequency
Batch-generating post drafts (threads, carousels, text posts) for a defined platform
Running a monthly strategy review: what to double down on, what to cut
Briefing an agent to produce a 4-week content calendar in JSON format
Real-time trend hijacking — requires human judgment on brand safety and timing
Community management and comment responses — relationship quality requires a human
Generating content for all platforms simultaneously from one prompt — quality degrades
Accounts under 1 month old with no ICP data — build ICP clarity first
When the goal is paid acquisition; use ppc-manager methodology instead

rule.statement: Master ONE platform before adding a second. Choose based on where your ICP already spends time, not where it is easiest to post.
rule.rationale: Follower migration across platforms is slow. Spreading across multiple platforms dilutes content quality and engagement time before momentum is built.

rule.statement: Score each platform 1-5 on four criteria: ICP audience present, content type match, competitor activity, organic reach potential. Pick the highest scorer.
rule.rationale: Subjective platform preferences lead to choosing the wrong channel. The scorecard forces objective audience-first thinking.

example.title: Platform scorecard for B2B SaaS founder
example.note: Twitter/X scores 18/20 (strong ICP, text content, active competitors, high organic reach). LinkedIn scores 14/20. Decision: start Twitter/X, add LinkedIn at 6 months.

antipattern.description: Choosing a platform because "everyone is on it" or because competitors are there without verifying ICP presence.
antipattern.reason: Competitors being on a platform does not mean your ICP converts there. Audience presence is the only criterion that matters at launch.

rule.statement: Define 3-5 content pillars with percentage allocations that sum to 100%. Reserve 10% for behind-the-scenes or personal content.
rule.rationale: Pillars prevent random posting and ensure audience knows what to expect. The 10% personal allocation humanizes the brand and builds trust.

example.title: SaaS founder content pillars
example.note: Product updates 40%, Industry insights 30%, Building in public 20%, Personal wins/fails 10%. Every content decision maps to a pillar.

rule.statement: Allocate at most 20% of posts to promotion. For accounts under 1K followers, use 95/5 value-to-promo ratio — trust is not yet established.
rule.rationale: Promotional content before trust is built causes unfollows. Value content compounds: followers stay and refer others.

section.name: posting-frequency

rule.statement: Batch content creation into 2-3 hour weekly sessions rather than creating single posts daily. Consistency improves and creative quality is higher in blocks.
rule.rationale: Daily single-post creation leads to inconsistency when other priorities intervene. Batching decouples creation from posting.

list.item: Instagram: minimum 3/week, optimal 1/day feed + daily Stories

section.reference: Monthly content calendar with pillar distribution and daily posting slots

rule.statement: Spend 5 minutes engaging with accounts you follow, 5 minutes replying to comments on your posts, and 5 minutes finding new accounts to engage with — daily.
rule.rationale: For accounts under 10K followers, engagement time drives more growth than posting frequency. If forced to choose, engage over post.

rule.statement: Reply to comments within 1 hour. Ask questions at the end of posts. DM new followers with an authentic message, not a pitch.
rule.rationale: Early comment engagement signals to algorithms that the post is generating conversation, boosting further distribution.

section.name: kpi-targets

rule.statement: Track engagement rate, not follower count, as the primary KPI for the first 6 months. Follower growth is a lagging indicator.
rule.rationale: Engagement rate reveals content quality and audience relevance. Follower count inflates via low-quality follows and does not predict conversion.

rule.statement: Engagement rate = (Likes + Comments + Shares) / Impressions * 100. Target: above 3% is good, above 6% is excellent.
rule.rationale: Rates below 1% indicate content-audience mismatch or posting to an unengaged audience. Rates above 3% signal the content resonates.

section.reference: Parse platform analytics CSV to compute avg, median, and top post engagement rates

antipattern.description: Spreading across too many platforms in the first 90 days.
antipattern.reason: Dilutes content quality and engagement time before momentum builds on any single platform.

antipattern.description: Giving up before 6 months of consistent posting.
antipattern.reason: Audience momentum is non-linear. Most accounts see exponential growth between months 4-8, not in the first 30 days.

antipattern.description: Using platform analytics only after paying for third-party tools.
antipattern.reason: Native platform analytics are free and sufficient for the first 6 months. Third-party tools add value only when cross-platform reporting becomes necessary.

antipattern.description: Repurposing content across platforms verbatim (e.g., Twitter thread copy-pasted to LinkedIn).
antipattern.reason: Each platform has distinct tone, format preferences, and algorithmic requirements. Verbatim cross-posting underperforms platform-native content by a significant margin.

rule.statement: Choose platform based on where the ICP already spends time, not where it is easiest to post — follower migration is slow.
rule.rationale: Distributing effort across multiple channels before mastering one leads to weak presence everywhere rather than strong presence on one platform.

rule.statement: Batch content creation into 2-3 hour weekly sessions rather than daily single-post creation; consistency improves and creative quality is higher in blocks.
rule.rationale: Batching decouples creation from posting and prevents quality degradation when other priorities intervene.

rule.statement: The 80/20 value-to-promo rule is minimum; for accounts under 1K followers, go 95/5 — trust is not yet established.
rule.rationale: Promotional content before trust is built causes unfollows. Value content compounds and builds audience loyalty.

rule.statement: Engagement time (5-5-5 method) drives more growth than posting frequency for accounts under 10K followers; if forced to choose, engage over post.
rule.rationale: Algorithm distribution favors accounts that generate early conversation and engagement on others' posts.

rule.statement: Track engagement rate, not follower count, as the primary KPI for the first 6 months — follower growth is a lagging indicator.
rule.rationale: Engagement rate reveals content quality and audience relevance; follower count is influenced by low-quality follows and third-party services.

rule.statement: Platform analytics are available natively and free; use them before paying for third-party tools.
rule.rationale: Native analytics provide sufficient data for strategy decisions in the first 6 months; paid tools add value only when cross-platform reporting is necessary.

section.name: agent-integration-notes

rule.statement: Run strategy in two phases: (1) planning — a single Sonnet call produces platform selection, pillar definitions, frequency targets, and KPI baselines; (2) execution — a Haiku call batch-generates post drafts.
rule.rationale: Separation of planning and execution allows human review and data injection between phases, improving content quality and alignment with brand voice.

rule.statement: Inject real engagement data monthly for a Sonnet strategy review. Human executes posting and engagement; agent handles content generation and analysis only.
rule.rationale: Agents cannot observe actual post performance or current platform dynamics; monthly feedback loops prevent stale recommendations.

section.p: Platform algorithm preferences change without documentation updates; agent recommendations on posting times and formats go stale within 3-6 months. Validate quarterly against native analytics.
Content pillar distribution (40/30/20/10) is a starting heuristic, not a law; real performance data overrides it in strategy reviews.
Cross-platform repurposing requires adaptation the agent often misses — a Twitter thread repurposed to LinkedIn verbatim underperforms. Run separate platform-specific prompts.
Engagement rate spikes from a viral post skew monthly averages; flag outliers before using data to adjust strategy.

template.purpose: Monthly content calendar with pillar distribution and weekly plan
Parse platform analytics CSV → avg/median engagement rate
